title: Basic Sentence Structure
description: Welcome to the ultimate rule of German: 'Verb is King'. Learn how to easily piece together beautiful, full sentences!
explanation:
**HOOK**
In English, we strictly follow the order: Who, Verb, What, When. 'I drink water today'. If you scramble it up, it sounds completely wrong! But German sentences are like Lego pieces—you can snap them on however you want, as long as you obey ONE absolute golden rule.

**CONCEPT**
We are learning how to piece together basic sentences smoothly—adding Time (when), Places (where), and People without sounding instantly like a robot.

**RULE**
Verb is King in Position 2! You can put almost ANYTHING at the very start of the sentence (time, place, the object), but the conjugated verb MUST hopelessly lock into the 2nd slot. If you put 'Today' in Position 1, the Subject ('I') has to jump firmly into Position 3 to make room for the Verb!

tables.title: The 'Verb is King' Map
tables.note: Look at Position 2! The verb 'trinke' is glued there no matter what you move around to the front. The subject almost always hugs right next to the verb.

deep_notes.title: WATCH OUT
deep_notes.text:
The absolute biggest mistake beginners make is word order for Time and Place. English throws time at the very end: 'I play soccer today.' 
German hates that! German likes Time as early as possible. You should say 'Ich spiele heute Fußball' (I play today soccer). Time always comes BEFORE Place!

examples.english: He learns German every day. (Time right after Verb!)
Tomorrow I play soccer. (Time first, Verb second, Subject third)
We are going home now. (Time 'jetzt' before Place 'Hause')

exercises.question: Welches Wort MUSS auf Position 2 in einem normalen Aussagesatz stehen?
exercises.explanation: The conjugated verb must always sit rigidly in Position 2.

exercises.hint: What is the 'ich' conjugation for trinken? Because 'Heute' is Pos 1, Verb is Pos 2.
exercises.explanation: trinken -> trinke. Verb is in Position 2!

exercises.question: Bilde den Satz (Tomorrow I play soccer):
exercises.hint: Morgen (Time) goes first. The verb stays second. Subject third.
exercises.explanation: Morgen (1) + spiele (Verb is King) + ich (Subj pushes to 3) + Fußball (4).

exercises.question: Wie ist die Regel für Zeit (Time) und Ort (Place) im Deutschen?
exercises.hint: German hates dealing with Time late.
exercises.explanation: German always puts Time before Place (e.g. Ich spiele HEUTE im Park).

exercises.question: Bilde den Satz (He comes home today):
Match the position for the sentence 'Morgen trinke ich Kaffee':

exercises.hint: Jeden Tag is Position 1. Now we need the 'sie' (she) version of the verb in Pos 2. (irregular!)

exercises.question: Welcher Satz ist KORREKT?
exercises.hint: Time (jeden Tag) should be pulled up early right after the verb. And don't push the Verb out of Position 2!
exercises.explanation: Verb in Pos 2 ('esse'). Time pulled up right after the Verb. (Never put time at the end like English!)

exercises.question: Bilde den Satz (Today the weather is nice):
exercises.hint: Time first. Verb (ist) second. Subject (das Wetter) third.
